issue_title: Can the look of jupyter notebook be customized in any way according to kernel status?
issue_body:
I have developed a [Script of Scripts](https://github.com/BoPeng/SOS) kernel that allows switching between different kernels using magics such as `%use R`, and `%with R`. It is working reasonably well but there is a small problem with the current implementation, namely there is no hint that which kernel is currently active. Is it possible to, for example, change the background of notebook according to current kernel? 

In addition, it would be useful if I can mark each cell with the kernel used so that I can get a notebook with cells in different colors. For example

```
[white SoS cell]
    import pandas as pd
    data = pd.read_excel('data.xls')
[purple R cell]
    %with R -i data -o m
    m <- apply(data, 1, median)
[white SoS cell]
    print(m)
```

I have a feeling that this could be done in javascript, but I currently have no idea how the browser (javascript) interacts with the kernel. Any suggestion or code example would be greatly appreciated.

issue_title: TimeStampType doesn't import
issue_body: TimeStampType doesn't define to_native, so it calls DateTimeType's to_native, which expects an ISO8601 string instead.

issue_title: Refactor of XUnit support
issue_body:
As everyone else realises how *awesome* XUnit support is with Jenkins, we need to refactor the code into something more sensible.

My plans are:

- an XUnitRerportBuilder class which has convenient methods like `.error()`, `.ok()`, `.skipped()`
- Proper XML construction rather than worrying about escaping various snippets.

XUnit and logging messages are increasingly filling very similar roles, so maybe there's a way we could handle both in one place? E.g. how `lint_ctx` gets tossed around, maybe we need to have a replacement `lint_ctx` which talks to TWO backends, the XURB backend and the `planemo.io` backend, dispatching messages to their appropriate locations.

Would that make sense @jmchilton? Are there places where this wouldn't be appropriate? Just wanted to have a short engineering discussion in this issue :)

issue_title: Write Spladder/Alt-Splice Pipeline
issue_body:
- variant calling ~ 1h / single thread / 1G RAM
- AS calling ~ 2.5 h / single thread / 5G RAM
- QC run ~10min / single thread / < 1G RAM

I would suggest to lump variant calling and QC together. 

As input data, I have currently used the hg38 Reference genome downloaded from ensembl and as annotation the Gencode V23 comprehensive. Let me know when you need more information. 

Full list of dependencies:

Variant calling step:
===============
Requirements:
* samtools - version 1.3

Pre-requisits:
* index genome.fa with: samtools faidx <genome.fa>
(this needs to be done only once and the genome.fa and the index can be provided as input files)

Call:
* samtools-1.3 mpileup -f [genome.fa] -l [position_file] -v [alignment.bam] -t DP,SP,INFO/AD,INFO/ADF,INFO/ADR,INFO/DPR,SP > [output.vcf]

Resources:
* time: ~ 1h
* memory: ~ 300MB
* disk space: only for result file ~ 10MB

Source:
http://www.htslib.org/download/

Alternative splicing step:
===================
Requirements:
* python 2.7
* pysam
* h5py
* numpy
* scipy

Pre-requisits:
* needs genome.fa
* needs annotation.gtf

Call:
* python spladder.py --insert_ir=y --insert_es=y --insert_ni=y --remove_se=n --validate_sg=n -b [alignment.bam] -o [outdir] -a [annotation.gtf] -v y -c 3 -M merge_graphs -T y -n [readlength] -P y -p n --sparse_bam y
(we can set the read length to constant 50 for now - this will only influence some filter criteria and should be fine - unless you have a large fraction of 32bp reads in your sets, which is unlikely)

Resources:
* time: ~ 2-3h
* memory: ~ 4 GB
* single CPU
* disk space: ~ 1GB	

Source:
https://github.com/ratschlab/spladder

QC tool:
=======
Requirements:
* python 2.7
* pysam
* pandas
* scipy
* numpy

Pre-requisits:
* needs pre-processed annotation table - will be provided by us

Call:
* checkBias_2.0.py -o [outfile-prefix] -n [alignment.bam] -v -a [annotation.gtf] -m [annotation.preprocessed.m53]

Resources:
* time: ~20min
* memory: < 1GB
* disk space: negligible

Source:
* https://github.com/KjongLehmann/m53

issue_title: Missing coverage for mixin class when testing derived class?
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is a bug or not.

Scenario:
I have an implementation of Levenberg-Marquard that I'm using to "invert" a function. Because I am interested in the broad category of all such functions (not just one particular form), I thought I'd be "clever" and implement this particular portion as a mixin that can be freely inherited by client classes. Unfortunately, the mixin itself is not directly testable (it requires that client classes provide certain key pieces of information, such as gradients and the forward operation which I am attempting to invert). 

When I look at test coverage reports, I see clear indication that I have run tests which exercise calls to the mixin's methods via its concrete subclasses / clients (I know because I currently have tests that fail after making these calls -- d'oh!). What is confusing to me is that when I look at the coverage reports, I see the entire mixin being marked as uncovered. 

What are reasonable expectations in this case? I can imagine an implementation that, were a test to fail, pytest / pytest-cov may drop any information related to that test (such as coverage) and that once those tests pass, I /should/ see the coverage on the base class? If not, what are the "right" ways to go about testing classes which are not directly instantiatable (e.g. mixins, traditional OO abstract base classes, ...) in a way that produces true estimates of test coverage for those classes? 

Thanks!

issue_title: Refactor Oscar Application to use Django AppConfig
issue_body:
# Description

By Django 1.11.8 (probably earlier, I have not checked) `startapp` generates an `apps.py` for housing application config classes. Related, `INSTALLED_APPS` integration is now documented as `<appname>.apps.<AppnameConfig>`, referencing these classes and this location.

Oscar also has an application config class, and it generally lives in `app.py`. There is much crossover between Oscar's app class and Django's app config class.

Oscar can probably configure itself from Django app config classes, if the Oscar Application can be designed as a mixin on a Django app config. Some fields should change, for example `self.app_name` > `self.name`. 

Also, I wonder if Django's model loaders could enhance or even replace all the dynamic class loading stuff in Oscar?

issue_title: Suggestion - lexical ordering and reflection.
issue_body:
Hi,

There's no official way to reflect on a type and get (say) public properties in their lexical (declared) order. Many articles and utilities rely on ordering by MetadataToken and although this seems to work, is sometimes regarded as risky and potentially breaking in a future release.

However lexical ordering is extremely useful and quite natural for many applications so providing a way to do this that has official support would be superb.

(For example we have Dapper utility code that formats SQL queries with {0} and {1} etc where parameters are expected and then use reflection to replace these with @<some_property_name> ensuring the SQL text always contains a parameter name that exactly matches the anonymous object's property name, 0 being the first property, 1 the second etc.).

I guess you could either flatly commit to honoring the MetadataToken and say that this will always be the same as the lexical order or perhaps introduce a new property on the Type class LexicalPosition where 0 indicates the first declared property, 1 the second and so on.

If this integer was assigned increasingly based on lexical order of members (whether they be fields, properties, public private etc) then this would work - getting all public properties ordered by LexicalPosition would always order correctly.

A new member could be added to **BindingFlags** - **LexicalOrder** to get stuff in lexical order, not setting this would cause it to behave as it does now - ensuring backward compatibility for existing code that uses reflection.

I'm posting this here because it is the compiler that will know this lexical order as it processes the token stream.

One could argue that writing code which depends on lexical order is risky but it would be no more risky than writing code that depends on a the spelling of a property.

Thanks.

(I originally posted this in the roslyn repo https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/issues/16733 but I guess it really requires changes lower down, although it would be the compiler that "knows" the lexical order (since it is aware of the token stream) it needs to persist that in the type info generated by the compilation process much as it does now with metadata token)

issue_title: API for using a MetaKernel-based kernel as a cell magic from IPython
issue_body:
Here is the code currently required to use a MetaKernel-based kernel (in this example, Calysto Processing) from inside IPython:

```python
from calysto.language.processing.kernel import ProcessingKernel

from IPython import get_ipython
from IPython.core.magic import register_cell_magic
from IPython.display import display

kernel = ProcessingKernel()   ## make a global Kernel
ip = get_ipython()                   ## need access to IPython session:
kernel.session = ip.parent.session
kernel.Display = display        ## use IPython's display, not MetaKernel's

@register_cell_magic
def processing(line, cell):
    "my cell magic"
    result = kernel.do_execute_direct(cell)
    return result
```

Then you can:

```
%%processing

void draw() {
    ellipse(50, 50, 10, 10);
}
```

It would be good to make this an official API. Should work with any MetaKernel-based kernel, yes?

```python
import folium
```

This error implies that only locally-accessible files are supported, but the documentation states only that:

I can obviously get around this using the `requests` library and passing `r.json()` to the `geo_str` parameter, but it would be more elegant and useful (as well as being consistent with the documentation) if that weren't actually necessary.

issue_title: Pickling Annotations can fail
issue_body:
Now that [cloudpickle is including annotations](https://github.com/cloudpipe/cloudpickle/commit/6d03ffe1b06d5abc8f8615ac57d475946aca4b38), there are certain `typing` classes that fail to pickle in versions of python < 3.7 that are now raising errors when included as annotations:

```python
## python 3.6.6

import typing
import cloudpickle

tt = typing.Union[str, typing.List[str]]
cloudpickle.dumps(tt, protocol=cloudpickle.DEFAULT_PROTOCOL) # always fails
# _pickle.PicklingError: Can't pickle typing.Union[str, typing.List[str]]: it's not the same object as typing.Union

def f(x: tt) -> tt:
    return x

## passes in cloudpickle==0.5.3 because type annotations were ignored
## but fails in 0.5.4+
cloudpickle.dumps(f, protocol=cloudpickle.DEFAULT_PROTOCOL)
```

All of this works fine in python 3.7

issue_title: Location Timezone support for python3 newstr
issue_body:
Things have been quiet for a while. Hope the fall is going well for all of you so far! 

The following code 

```
from __future__ import (absolute_import, division,
                        print_function, unicode_literals)
from builtins import *
from pvlib.location import Location
tz = -10
str_tz = 'Etc/GMT+' + str(-1*int(tz))
my_loc = Location(20.0, -155.9, str_tz, 105.0, "my_place")
```

gives the following exception:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/lucas/Documents/Code/sim/pv.py", line 52, in <module>
    my_loc = Location(20.0, -155.9, str_tz, 105.0, "my_place")
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pvlib/location.py", line 76, in __init__
    raise TypeError('Invalid tz specification')
TypeError: Invalid tz specification
```

issue_title: Documentation request: inc_ref and memory consumption
Consider adding a `(get-objectives)` command
issue_body:
As discussed [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/1101#issuecomment-310680676) and [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/1101#issuecomment-310686584)

Currently when we have an optimization problem Z3 prints `sat` followed by `objectives`. It might be nicer to stick to one s-expr per response rule that SMTLib2 adheres to, and for good reason. To alleviate, perhaps the two should be separated, and a new command `(get-objectives)` as suggested by @wintersteiger might be a better choice.

Note that this would also unify the behavior of `lexicographic`, `boxed` and `pareto` modes: The first two do print objectives, but the last one doesn't. By implementing `(get-objectives)` as a separate command, Z3 can properly respond to the first two and say something appropriate for the `pareto` case.

issue_title: How does one extend Z3 with new solvers / theories?
issue_body:
Hello,

I was interested in understanding what the process is to extend Z3's theory, and add additional solvers for existing theories. Googling led me to StackOverflow answers which states that the plugin based architecture is deprecated.

I can't seem to find any up-to-date information about how to extend Z3.

For some context, I'm interested in plugging in [`isl` (integer set library)](http://isl.gforge.inria.fr/) into Z3 as a new solver for linear / affine constraints, and also expose some of the "extra" functionality that ISL provides, but I have no idea how to start.

Thanks!

issue_title: Unexpected value change in multiple calls to check-sat
issue_body:
I'm observing something I haven't expected, using a freshly checked out copy of z3.

For this input:

```
(set-logic QF_BV)
(declare-fun s0 () (_ BitVec 8))
(check-sat)
(assert (bvsge s0 #x05))
(assert (bvslt s0 #x07))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s0))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s0))
```

Z3 says:

```
sat
sat
((s0 #x06))
sat
((s0 #x05))
```

Now, there's nothing really wrong here, as z3 is producing a valid value in each case. But I'm surprised to see that the value is changing between the second and the third call to `check-sat`, even though nothing changes in between.

The above script looks "weird" indeed, but it's the minimum I was able to reduce from a more complicated scenario.

Furthermore, if I use `Int` for the type (as opposed to `(_ BitVec 8)`), the model remains the same.

Is this the expected behavior? While there isn't anything that says successive calls to `check-sat` with no assertions in between need not produce the same model, I'm surprised to see that it indeed does.

```shell
         },
         {

            "icmp_type" : "",
            "position" : "2",
            "protocol" : "tcp",
            "source_address_end" : "",
            "source_address_start" : "",
            "source_port_end" : "",
            "source_port_start" : ""
         },
         {
            "action" : "reject",
            "comment" : "",
            "destination_address_end" : "",
            "destination_address_start" : "",
            "destination_port_end" : "",
            "destination_port_start" : "",
            "direction" : "in",
            "family" : "",
            "icmp_type" : "",

            "protocol" : "",

            "source_address_start" : "",
            "source_port_end" : "",
            "source_port_start" : ""
         }
      ]

}
```

However, the API client doesn't seem to have support for the new attribute yet. At the moment, the lack of support breaks the [upcloud_firewall.py](https://github.com/UpCloudLtd/upcloud-ansible/blob/master/modules/upcloud_firewall.py) Ansible module you guys have made, since it wants to check the existing firewall rules via the API first.

A quick fix I found was to add a new `"comment": None` attribute to the `attributes` hash in [upcloud_api/firewall.py](https://github.com/UpCloudLtd/upcloud-python-api/blob/2ee612bdf40579a840e8f943b5c880b157cec13e/upcloud_api/firewall.py). issue_title: Sessions in Manage Endpoints and Manage sessions not in synch
issue_body: The sessions in manage endpoints tab are not in synch with the sessions in manage sessions tab in widget UI as show below. The session 6 has been deleted from manage endpoints as shown below, but the UI continues to display the session in the endpoint list. Even the manage sessions UI displays the deleted session as below: This is primarily due to the methods that are used in spark controller. ### Method called when deleting session from manage endpoint widget _Class: Spark Controller_ `delete_session_by_id(endpoint, session_id) -calls-> creates a new LivySession object, say ls with LivyReliableHTTPClient and does ls.delete()` This method doesn't go through the session manager route and hence the sessions in session manager continue to exist even after being deleted. The status shown in manage sessions tab ### Method called when deleting from manage sessions widget _Class: Spark Controller_ `delete_session_by_name(name) -calls-> session_manager.delete_client(name)` This method cleanly deletes the session by removing session from the sessions managed by session manager. So, to make the experience consistent the method **delete_session_by_id** needs to be updated to go through session manager route.

issue_title: Benchmark mode cy/CL output for Himeno is off by a factor of 2
issue_body:
emmy$ kerncraft -m machine-files/IvyBridgeEP_E5-2660v2.yml --pmodel Benchmark -D L 50 -D M 500 -D N 500 --cache-predictor LC --compiler icc kernels/himeno.c
[...]
Runtime (per cacheline update): 120.84 cy/CL
MEM volume (per repetition): 760030000 Byte
Performance: 4952.08 MFLOP/s
Performance: 145.65 MLUP/s
Performance: 145.65 It/s

The performance output (4952 MFLOP/s) does not match the runtime per CL output:

(16 LUPs / 121 cy) * 32 FLOP/LUP * 2.2 Gcy/s = 9.89 GFLOP/s

This is exactly twice the reported performance number above. Based on my own benchmarks (and the performance analysis provided by the ECM model in kerncraft) I conclude that the cy/CL number is too small by a factor of 2 but the performance output is correct. I suspect this has to to with the Himeno benchmark using single-precision data.

issue_title: Any example using variables?
only_fields does not exclude fields not listed in it
issue_body:
I have a django 1.10 project with the following packages installed and cannot seem to get only fields preventing the query of a user's password, which **is not in only_fields**.

Is this something goofy with the Django User model or did I miss something?

```
django-filter==0.14.0
django-graphiql==0.4.4
graphene==0.10.2
graphql-core==0.5.3
graphql-django-view==1.3
graphql-relay==0.4.4
```

The schema:

```py
"""Graphene schemas.

The schema.query is exposed under the endpoint /graphql
"""
import graphene
from graphene import ObjectType, with_context
from graphene.contrib.django.filter import DjangoFilterConnectionField
from graphene.contrib.django.types import DjangoNode

from django.contrib.auth.models import User


class UserNode(DjangoNode):
    class Meta:
        model = User
        only_fields = ('first_name', 'last_name', 'email')
        filter_fields = ['first_name', 'last_name', 'email']
        filter_order_by = ['first_name']


class Query(ObjectType):
    me = DjangoFilterConnectionField(UserNode)

    @with_context
    def resolve_me(self, args, context, info):
        """Return the user's info."""
        # context will reference to the Django request
```

issue_title: Feature: assume cross account role
issue_body:
Congrats! This is a useful tool 👍  

Though we could improve it for cross-account AWS setup. 

Some might find it easier to manage federated SSO if it is mapped to few roles of `Accounts` AWS account (`X` on diagram). Other AWS accounts roles are mapped to the `Accounts'` roles, eg: `Dev` roles on `Staging` and `Production` accounts are have cross-account trust to `Dev` role on `X`, etc. That way only few values needs to be pushed to google user profiles and it scales if number of AWS accounts increases.

Diagram:
```
# Currently supported multirole through custom musti-value profile field:
# --role-arn A
               -> { Option A   -> AWS Acc X, Role A
Google SSO App -> { Option B   -> AWS Acc X, Role B
               -> { Option C   -> AWS Acc X, Role C
                  
#Proposed for cross account setup
# --role-arn Dev --xa-role-arn Z-Dev [--xa-role-profile]
                                                            -> { Acc Y, Role Dev
Google SSO App -> { Option Dev   -> AWS Acc X, Role Dev     -> { Acc Z, Role Dev

                                                            -> { Acc Y, Role Admin
               -> { Option Admin -> AWS Acc X, Role Admin   -> { Acc Z, Role Admin
```

With current state of the tool to assume cross-account role for CLI requires something like:
```bash
aws-google-auth -a --sp-id 123456 --idp-id C01example --username user@example.com

aws sts assume-role --role-arn "arn:aws:iam::123455678:role/Dev" --profile sts

# here you need to parse output of the prev command and, for example, export it to env:

export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=XKLKJLDFLDKLF
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="EXAMPLESECRET"
export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN="TOKENFOO"
```

It be nice to add feature to assume sts credentials for `--xa-role-arn` option and write down profile to config `--xa-role-profile`.

issue_title: Common Name inconsistent with --webroot-map
issue_body:
When the user invokes `certbot` with `-d`, the Common Name field is set to the first domain passed via that argument. `webroot-map`, which is essentially a combination of `-d` and `-w`, does not seem to follow this behavior and sets the Common Name to a (seemingly) random domain from the map. It would make sense for this to be consistent with `-d`, or at least to document this behaviour somewhere.

Sample `config.ini` to replicate:

    webroot-map	= {"example.com,1.example.com,2.example.com":"/var/www/html", "3.example.com":"/var/www/html", "4.example.com":"/var/www/html"}

Expected CN: `example.com`
Actual CN: `4.example.com`

Originally reported [here](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/set-default-cn-name-for-multiple-certificate/21625/5?u=pfg) (includes a workaround).

issue_title: Deprecation warnings for old datalab namespace
issue_body:
It may be helpful to add deprecation warnings for functions in the old `datalab` namespace to encourage users to move to `google.datalab`. At the very least, the deprecation warning could refer users to the `google.datalab` documentation. My feeling is that by adding warnings there will be fewer complaints from users when the old `datalab` namespace is removed.

This is just a suggestion. Please feel free to close this issue.

issue_title: Provide a way to remove whitespace (including newlines)
issue_body:
--indent=none option already removes whitespace but not newlines. It would be helpful to be able to remove _all_ insignificant whitespace from the file.

------------------------------------
Imported from Launchpad using lp2gh.

 * date created: 2011-01-19T21:26:02Z
 * owner: kangax
 * the launchpad url was https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/705127

issue_title: Roadmap for pytrip
issue_body:
In steps:
1. separate pytrip from pytripgui #9 
each project will have separate release numbering
2. make new release of pytrip lib (not release gui)
3. heavily refactor pytrip lib to fix all PEP8 errors (formatting)
4. add tests and docs to pytrip lib
5. make new release of pytrip lib (not release gui)
6. fix GUI, assuming it will work with some old python version. Might be ugly but should work.

issue_title: include vdx example
issue_body:
simple example which:
- loads a CTX cube
- loads a VDX file
- print a list of all available contours
- selects by mask one contour
- sets all HU units to zero
- saves new CTX cube to file

issue_title: Remote target file name derivation
issue_body:
Here is a MWE:

```
[default]
input: "start.txt"
output: "${input[0]!n}.done"
task:
run:
  sleep 5
  echo completed > ${output}
```

and I run with `-r` I get this error from the string interpolation routine:

```
INFO: Executing default_0: 
INFO: input:    [remote("start.txt")]
ERROR: Failed to execute workflow default
[default_0] RuntimeError:
	Failed to process step output: "${input[0]!n}.done" (expected str, bytes or os.PathLike object, not remote)
[default] RuntimeError:
	1 failed step: default_0
```

issue_title: Add a title to the panel
issue_body: We should add a title (scratch) to the panel so that users know what this panel is about. We can make it a tab (although the only one) to make it easier for us to add more tabs later on.

issue_title: SoS homepage is not mobile friendly.
issue_body:
When I view the homepage on my mobile phone, there are two problems:

1. header image is too tall.
2. The pages are displayed within the screen but can be scrolled left or right.

issue_title: Generate changelog based on the issues of the milestone
issue_body:
In gitlab by @sylvaingaudan on Jul 20, 2017, 08:14

When releasing a version, check whether a milestone for this version exists or not. If it does, then extract the issues and log them (id, title and tags) and append them at the top of the existing CHANGELOG.md

issue_title: possible to add keys?
issue_body:
Is it possible to add keys to the Trie after its been created? I've seen the restore facility (eg: `trie.restore_key(1)`) and looked into the source but haven't seen anything offering this. Something like

t = Trie([u'one'])
t._add_key(u'two')

issue_title: Backend for Microsoft Graph
issue_body: The Microsoft Graph API is currently not supported by any backend. With a new backend similar to AzureAD i got it to work using social-app-django.

issue_title: Error using the Jupyter notebook command
issue_body:
<!--
General questions should be asked on the mailing list ray-dev@googlegroups.com.

Before submitting an issue, please fill out the following form.
-->

### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: MacOS 10.12
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: pip3 install ray
- **Ray version**: 0.3.1
- **Python version**: 3.6
- **Exact command to reproduce**: see below

### Describe the problem
I just tried the new instructions for remote Jupyter notebooks that Philipp told me about. Following the docs (i.e., creating a cluster with `ray create_or_update` and the config file below), I ran the following command after brining up a cluster:

`ssh -L 8899:localhost:8899 -i /Users/ludwig/.ssh/ray-autoscaler_us-west-2.pem ubuntu@34.213.245.91 jupyter notebook --port=8899`

The notebook server starts, but there are a few error messages:

```
[I 01:26:30.013 NotebookApp] Starting initial scan of virtual environments...
[E 01:26:30.015 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
[I 01:26:48.188 NotebookApp] 302 GET /?token=9fc6dfe9e6ae8aaff5b3fc1759189d82a7bb97c9d2a5a3c9 (127.0.0.1) 0.79ms
[E 01:26:50.548 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
[E 01:26:50.702 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
[E 01:26:50.808 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
[I 01:26:59.413 NotebookApp] Creating new notebook in
[I 01:26:59.438 NotebookApp] Writing notebook-signing key to /home/ubuntu/.local/share/jupyter/notebook_secret
[E 01:27:01.730 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
[E 01:27:01.733 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
[E 01:27:01.735 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
[E 01:27:02.586 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
[E 01:27:02.603 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
[I 01:27:02.610 NotebookApp] Kernel started: 2be43b15-d11c-4eb1-ba24-3a17b3fda0b4
[I 01:27:03.717 NotebookApp] Adapting to protocol v5.1 for kernel 2be43b15-d11c-4eb1-ba24-3a17b3fda0b4
[I 01:29:01.892 NotebookApp] Saving file at /Untitled.ipynb
[E 01:29:23.252 NotebookApp] 'conda' not found in path.
```
Trying to import Ray in a new notebook then yields

`ImportError: No module named 'ray'`

Is this to be expected / did I do something wrong?


### Source code / logs

```
# An unique identifier for the head node and workers of this cluster.
cluster_name: ludwig_test_1

# The minimum number of workers nodes to launch in addition to the head
# node. This number should be >= 0.
min_workers: 4

# The maximum number of workers nodes to launch in addition to the head
# node. This takes precedence over min_workers.
max_workers: 4

# The autoscaler will scale up the cluster to this target fraction of resource
# usage. For example, if a cluster of 10 nodes is 100% busy and
# target_utilization is 0.8, it would resize the cluster to 13. This fraction
# can be decreased to increase the aggressiveness of upscaling.
target_utilization_fraction: 0.8

# If a node is idle for this many minutes, it will be removed.
idle_timeout_minutes: 5

# Cloud-provider specific configuration.
provider:
    type: aws
    region: us-west-2
    availability_zone: us-west-2c

# How Ray will authenticate with newly launched nodes.
auth:
    ssh_user: ubuntu
# By default Ray creates a new private keypair, but you can also use your own.
# If you do so, make sure to also set "KeyName" in the head and worker node
# configurations below.
#    ssh_private_key: /path/to/your/key.pem

# Provider-specific config for the head node, e.g. instance type. By default
# Ray will auto-configure unspecified fields such as SubnetId and KeyName.
# For more documentation on available fields, see:
# http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#EC2.ServiceResource.create_instances
head_node:
    InstanceType: m5.large
    ImageId: ami-3b6bce43  # Amazon Deep Learning AMI (Ubuntu)

    # You can provision additional disk space with a conf as follows
    # BlockDeviceMappings:
    #     - DeviceName: /dev/sda1
    #       Ebs:
    #           VolumeSize: 100

    # Additional options in the boto docs.

# Provider-specific config for worker nodes, e.g. instance type. By default
# Ray will auto-configure unspecified fields such as SubnetId and KeyName.
# For more documentation on available fields, see:
# http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#EC2.ServiceResource.create_instances
worker_nodes:
    InstanceType: m5.large
    ImageId: ami-3b6bce43  # Amazon Deep Learning AMI (Ubuntu)

    # Run workers on spot by default. Comment this out to use on-demand.
    InstanceMarketOptions:
        MarketType: spot
        # Additional options can be found in the boto docs, e.g.
        #   SpotOptions:
        #       MaxPrice: MAX_HOURLY_PRICE

    # Additional options in the boto docs.

# Files or directories to copy to the head and worker nodes. The format is a
# dictionary from REMOTE_PATH: LOCAL_PATH, e.g.
file_mounts: {
#    "/path1/on/remote/machine": "/path1/on/local/machine",
#    "/path2/on/remote/machine": "/path2/on/local/machine",
}

# List of shell commands to run to set up nodes.
setup_commands:
    # Note: if you're developing Ray, you probably want to create an AMI that
    # has your Ray repo pre-cloned. Then, you can replace the pip installs
    # below with a git checkout <your_sha> (and possibly a recompile).
    - pip install -U ray==0.3.1

# Custom commands that will be run on the head node after common setup.
head_setup_commands:
    - pip install boto3==1.4.8  # 1.4.8 adds InstanceMarketOptions

# Custom commands that will be run on worker nodes after common setup.
worker_setup_commands: []

# Command to start ray on the head node. You don't need to change this.
head_start_ray_commands:
    - ray stop
    - ray start --head --redis-port=6379 --autoscaling-config=~/ray_bootstrap_config.yaml

# Command to start ray on worker nodes. You don't need to change this.
worker_start_ray_commands:
    - ray stop
    - ray start --redis-address=$RAY_HEAD_IP:6379
```

issue_title: add fluent interface support to all methods
issue_body:
Please add support to all (or most) ipywidgets methods for a _simple_ fluent interface.  That would allow method chaining.  From my perspective, a simple fluent interface means that instance methods that normally do not return a value should return a reference to the instance instead.

For example, the current `Button.on_click` implementation (docstring omitted) is:

```python
    def on_click(self, callback, remove=False):
        self._click_handlers.register_callback(callback, remove=remove)
```

To add simple fluent interface support, place a new `return self` statement line at the end of the method.  The updated implementation would be:

```python
    def on_click(self, callback, remove=False):
        self._click_handlers.register_callback(callback, remove=remove)
        return self
```

The benefit is that code that uses ipywidgets could be shortened.  For example, code to instantiate a Button with the current ipywidgets:

```python
button = widgets.Button(description='Do Something')
button.on_click(doSomething)
display(button)
```

With simple fluent interface support, it could be shortened to:

```python
display(widgets.Button(description='Do Something').on_click(doSomething))
```

That will give notebook authors the option of saving space in their documents.

----

FWIW, if fluent interface support were added throughout ipywidgets (i.e., in this case, to `widget.CallbackDispatcher.register_callback`), the implementation could be simply the addition of the `return` keyword to the current code:

```python
    def on_click(self, callback, remove=False):
        return self._click_handlers.register_callback(callback, remove=remove)
```

issue_title: Consider renaming `lazy_errors`
issue_body:
In implementing the class-based configuration, I am writing docstring entries for things that we currently just have a longer description in the docs for. One of these is `lazyErrors`. In the [docs](http://parsl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/userguide/exceptions.html?highlight=lazyErrors#lazy-fail), we have this description titled `Lazy Fail` (even though the option is called 'lazyErrors'):
> While Retries address resiliency at the level of Apps, lazy failure adds resiliency at the workflow level. When lazy failures are enabled, the workflow does not halt as soon as it encounters a failure, but continues execution of every app that is unaffected. Lazy failures is the default behavior in parsl, with the expectation that when running production workflows, individual app failures can be deferred until the end of the workflow. During the development and testing of workflows, failing immediately on any failure is often preferred and this behavior is possible by setting lazyErrors=False.

I do not favor the name `lazy_fail` or `lazy_errors`. I'm not sure the lazy part is a perfect description of what we're doing, and I think it relies on background knowledge unnecessarily. I propose `halt_on_error` or `halt_on_failure`. In that way, the name would describe what is being done without assuming prior knowledge or a mental mapping between concepts. The docstring entry could be (I'm open to help tweaking this):
> Halt workflow processing and exit immediately if any apps fails.

We've been discussing batching all of the user-facing changes together, which is why I wanted to address this now.

issue_title: Cannot configure S3 Event Notifications
issue_body:
Hi,

When trying to configure S3 Event Notifications using [Terraform](https://www.terraform.io/), the request is ignored by localstack.
The cause of the problem is how localstack parses query strings from the URL in the [s3_listener.py#L451](https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/services/s3/s3_listener.py#L451).
Terraform submits request to URL like `http://localhost:4572/my-bucket?notification=` but localstack does not handle query parameters with empty values.
The fix is simple, [line 451 of s3_listener.py](https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/services/s3/s3_listener.py#L451) should be changed from `query_map = urlparse.parse_qs(query)` to `query_map = urlparse.parse_qs(query,keep_blank_values=1)`

Alessandro

issue_title: rdflib plugins should self register
issue_body:
eike...@gmail.com, 2008-01-27T19:31:59.000Z 
Currently plugin.py contains the calls to register for all the plugins that ship with rdflib. Each plugin 
should call this

Comment 1 by eike...@gmail.com
Didn't really mean self register, but rather that rdflib.plug module itself shouldn't be registering them.

Plugs can now be registered using pkg_resources entry points. They can also be registered by calling the 
register function in rdflib.plugin. See help(rdflib.plugin) for more information.
